schools.summary: Gill: they had more cattle in proportion than the other tribes, perhaps more given to the pastoral life; the land of Jazer and Gilead, east of Jordan, was a place for cattle.

themes: the children of Reuben and... Gad had a very great multitude of cattle... the place was a place for cattle
drawn by present advantage
tempted to stop short of the better inheritance

schools.summary: Gill: Gad is mentioned first though Reuben was the firstborn, perhaps because the Gadites were the first authors of the scheme, moving it to the Reubenites who lay encamped by them.

themes: The children of Gad and the children of Reuben came and spake unto Moses
Gad the likely authors of the scheme
a fair-seeming request, its spirit yet to be tested

schools.summary: Gill: these places belonged to the Amorites and were taken from Sihon their king; Jazer stood about fifteen miles from Heshbon, the capital.
Clarke: identifies the towns of the region — Ataroth, Dibon, and the rest.

themes: Ataroth, and Dibon, and Jazer... and Nebo, and Beon
the lately-conquered Amorite country
the near and sure preferred to the land beyond

schools.summary: Gill: the conquest of this country is ascribed to the Lord, for the victory was his; he smote their enemies and delivered the land into Israel's hands; the land was a land for cattle.

themes: the country which the LORD smote... is a land for cattle, and thy servants have cattle
owning God's hand yet bending it to gain
the gift valued for its worldly convenience

schools.summary: Gill: they spake to Moses in a modest, respectful manner.
Ellicott: 'bring us not over Jordan' may mean not to settle there or not to cross at all.
Clarke: they asked it because the land was good for pasturage.

themes: let this land be given unto thy servants for a possession, and bring us not over Jordan
content to settle short of Canaan
the look of covetousness and cowardice

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was displeased, the request having at first sight the appearance of covetousness and cowardice. Calvin: so sharp a reproof shows the greatness of the wrong; Moses was not carried away by inconsiderate warmth nor fell into error, but was rightly angered.

themes: Shall your brethren go to war, and shall ye sit here?
a righteous anger at a real wrong
the bond of brotherhood broken by seeking ease (Gal. 6:2)

schools.summary: Gill: their settling on that side Jordan would lose Israel two tribes' help, and might be thought to proceed from selfish views and love of ease, setting a bad example and disheartening the rest from going over.

themes: wherefore discourage ye the heart of the children of Israel from going over into the land
the self-seeking of some chilling the many
no man's ease his own affair alone

schools.summary: Gill: Moses means not their fathers only but all the tribes, who did much such a part — not choosing to go into the good land, but sending spies first who brought an ill report and discouraged the people.

themes: Thus did your fathers, when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea to see the land
the old refusal at Kadesh recalled
the old unbelief in a new dress

schools.summary: Gill: the valley or brook of Eshcol was so called from the cluster of grapes they cut down and brought thence; they discouraged the people from entering the land God had given.

themes: when they went up unto the valley of Eshcol... they discouraged the heart of the children of Israel
full evidence of God's gift met with unbelief
the fruit in hand, a faithless report on the lips

schools.summary: Gill: the anger was against the spies who brought the ill report and against all the people who were disheartened and murmured; the LORD sware an oath in his anger.

themes: the LORD's anger was kindled the same time, and he sware
God's grave displeasure at the unbelief
a settled wrath sworn against the discouragers

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the sentence already recorded (Num. 14:29) on those from twenty years old and upward; the reason given is that they had not wholly followed God.

themes: none of the men that came up out of Egypt... shall see the land... because they have not wholly followed me
the divided heart their undoing
God requiring a whole-hearted following

schools.summary: Gill: discusses whether Caleb or his father is called the Kenezite, inclining to the latter; the reason of their preservation is that they had wholly followed the LORD.

themes: Save Caleb... and Joshua... for they have wholly followed the LORD
the two faithful inheriting where the multitude fell
whole-hearted following rare and rewarded

schools.summary: Gill: the anger was for murmuring against the report of the spies.
Ellicott: Moses declares the fulfilment of the prediction announced at the time of the spies (Num. 14:33-34).

themes: he made them wander in the wilderness forty years, until all the generation... was consumed
the long judgment of unbelief
the word fulfilled (Num. 14:33-34)

schools.summary: Gill: they had succeeded their fathers in families and substance, and he suggests in their evil dispositions also. Calvin: they were 'filling up the measure' of their fathers, as Christ spoke of the Jews of his time, augmenting the fierce anger of the LORD.

themes: ye are risen up in your fathers' stead, an increase of sinful men, to augment yet the fierce anger of the LORD
filling up the measure of the fathers
the old unbelief renewed (Matt. 23:32)

schools.summary: Gill: turning away means ceasing to follow God in his commandments and worship, should they stop short and not go over Jordan.
Keil & Delitzsch: if they turned away, God would yet again leave Israel in the wilderness.

themes: if ye turn away from after him, he will yet again leave them in the wilderness; and ye shall destroy all this people
one part's sin able to ruin the whole
no man sinning to himself alone

schools.summary: Gill: the heads of Gad and Reuben came a little nearer to Moses, having more to say to explain their meaning, using some freedom and boldness.
Keil & Delitzsch: they proposed to build sheepfolds for their cattle and cities for their little ones.

themes: We will build sheepfolds here for our cattle, and cities for our little ones
drawing near to explain their meaning
their hearts not so selfish as first appeared

schools.summary: Gill: they said this to free themselves from the charge of cowardice, willing to put on their armour and expose their lives for their brethren.
Keil & Delitzsch: they would go armed before the children of Israel.

themes: we ourselves will go ready armed before the children of Israel, until we have brought them unto their place
leading the van of the battle
the charge of cowardice answered with foremost courage

schools.summary: Gill: they would not return to their houses, families, or substance, but leave them behind and not think of returning until all Israel was settled.

themes: We will not return unto our houses, until the children of Israel have inherited every man his inheritance
forgoing their own rest for their brethren
a pattern of brotherly faithfulness

schools.summary: Gill: they said this not as despising the good land but giving up all pretensions to it should they be settled where they desired; not of so selfish and covetous a disposition as to desire a share on both sides.

themes: we will not inherit with them on yonder side Jordan... our inheritance is fallen to us on this side Jordan
content with their lot, not despising the land
contentment, not greed

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was now better disposed and more satisfied with the reasonableness of their request, it being explained.
Ellicott: the same verb and preposition as before; the army of Israel was regarded as the army of the LORD, going 'before the LORD.'

themes: If ye will do this thing, if ye will go armed before the LORD to war
Moses accepting on a fair condition
the war God's own, their faithfulness a duty to him

schools.summary: Gill: Moses tries them thoroughly, requiring them not only to march but to go over Jordan, not some but all, and that before the LORD, the ark being the symbol of his presence, until the LORD had driven out his enemies.

themes: will go all of you armed over Jordan before the LORD, until he hath driven out his enemies
the whole duty bound without reserve
the LORD the conqueror, the tribes his instruments

schools.summary: Gill: the land subdued 'before the LORD' means the enemies conquered in his presence, by his power, for his people.
Ellicott: the fulfilment of the grant (Deut. 3:12-20; Josh. 13:15-33). Wesley: 'before the LORD,' in his sight and service.

themes: ye shall return, and be guiltless before the LORD, and before Israel
a clear conscience toward God and man
integrity satisfying both heaven and the brethren (2 Cor. 8:21)

schools.summary: Gill: this is if they will not do as they promised and as Moses insisted; the breaking of their vow would be a sin not against man only but against the LORD, which would surely find them out.

themes: if ye will not do so... ye have sinned against the LORD: and be sure your sin will find you out
the certainty of sin's discovery
no sin hidden, every work judged (Eccl. 12:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the cities and folds were for the safety of their little ones and sheep, as proposed; they were to do that which had proceeded out of their mouth, to keep their vow.

themes: Build you cities for your little ones, and folds for your sheep; and do that which hath proceeded out of your mouth
the keeping of the vow pressed
the spoken pledge a debt to pay (Deut. 23:23)

schools.summary: Gill: they answered his speech, agreeing both to march before the LORD to battle and to provide for their children and flocks, submitting wholly to the terms.

themes: Thy servants will do as my lord commandeth
full and willing submission to the terms
binding themselves to the whole duty by free consent

schools.summary: Gill: these were their families and their substance, to remain in the cities of Gilead while the men went to war.

themes: Our little ones, our wives, our flocks, and all our cattle, shall be there in the cities of Gilead
the families and substance left behind
their dearest committed to God's keeping

schools.summary: Gill: they would pass over the river Jordan and go into the land of Canaan; every man armed, before the LORD, to battle.

themes: thy servants will pass over, every man armed for war, before the LORD to battle
the pledge restated plainly
their resolve to bear their full part past mistaking

schools.summary: Gill: Moses determined the case, perhaps by the vote and consent of the whole court, and strictly enjoined its observance.
Henry: Moses settled the matter with Eleazar and Joshua his successor, knowing he should not live to see it performed.

themes: Moses commanded Eleazar the priest, and Joshua... and the chief fathers
the charge handed to his successors
the covenant secured beyond his lifetime

schools.summary: Gill: Moses spake to Eleazar, Joshua, and the princes.
Keil & Delitzsch: if they pass over armed before the LORD and the land be subdued, give them the land of Gilead for a possession.

themes: If the children of Gad and... Reuben will pass with you over Jordan... then ye shall give them the land of Gilead
the grant made conditional and binding
the possession earned by faithful service

schools.summary: Gill: this is if they should change their minds, break their promise, and refuse to go over armed.
Wesley: they should then forfeit their possessions in Gilead and be constrained to seek possessions in Canaan among their brethren.

themes: if they will not pass over with you armed, they shall have possessions among you in the land of Canaan
the privilege tied to the duty
the reward withheld for breach of faith

schools.summary: Gill: the word is singular, showing their unanimity, all replying as one man, or one speaking for all.
Wesley: 'as the LORD hath said,' either now by Moses' mouth or formerly where he commanded such mutual help.

themes: As the LORD hath said unto thy servants, so will we do
the tribes answering as one man
the terms owned as God's command

schools.summary: Gill: this is repeated again and again, for confirmation, assuring it should be strictly performed.
Keil & Delitzsch: pass over armed before the LORD, that the possession of our inheritance on this side Jordan may be ours.

themes: We will pass over armed before the LORD into the land of Canaan, that... our inheritance... may be ours
the pledge sealed beyond doubt
the gift secured by the duty kept

schools.summary: Gill: Moses gave it by word in the court, or by some instrument drawn up and signed by him and the sanhedrim.
Ellicott: the first mention of Manasseh, the original application having been only by Gad and Reuben; half Manasseh now joined.

themes: Moses gave... to the children of Gad, and... Reuben, and unto half the tribe of Manasseh... the kingdom of Sihon... and... Og
the east-Jordan land formally granted
half Manasseh added to fill the conquered land

schools.summary: Gill: they rather repaired the walls and rebuilt houses demolished in the wars with Sihon and Og.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Gadites restored and fortified Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer.

themes: And the children of Gad built Dibon
the towns of Sihon and Og repaired
the two tribes settling their portion

schools.summary: Gill: of these no mention is made elsewhere except Jaazer, the same with Jazer.
Keil & Delitzsch: continue the list of Gadite towns — Atroth, Shophan, Jaazer, Jogbehah.

themes: And Atroth
the towns of Sihon and Og repaired
the two tribes settling their portion

schools.summary: Gill: Bethnimrah is the same as Nimrah, and Bethharan the same as Betharam, later called Livias or Julias by Herod; these the Gadites built or repaired for their families, as fenced cities with sheepfolds.

themes: Bethnimrah, and Bethharan, fenced cities: and folds for sheep
walled towns and folds for safety
the Gadites keeping their own proposal

schools.summary: Gill: Heshbon was the royal city of Sihon, Elealeh within a mile of it; these were cities long before, only repaired, for they had not time to build new, passing over Jordan in a few months.

themes: And the children of Reuben built Heshbon
the towns of Sihon and Og repaired
the two tribes settling their portion

schools.summary: Gill: Nebo was the name of an idol, and Baalmeon means 'Baal's habitation,' likely a temple of his; the Reubenites, not liking to retain such idolatrous names, gave them others.

themes: Nebo, and Baalmeon, (their names being changed,)... and gave other names unto the cities
a pious separation from idolatry
not so much as naming false gods (Exod. 23:13)

schools.summary: Gill: this was the part of Gilead Moses gave to half Manasseh, till now in the hand of the Amorites; half Mount Gilead and its cities had been given to Reuben and Gad, the part already conquered.

themes: the children of Machir... went to Gilead, and took it, and dispossessed the Amorite
their portion won by their own conquest
the valour of Manasseh's sons

schools.summary: Gill: this was to the children of Machir who went and took it; some think Machir himself was alive, but that is not probable, for all over twenty at the Exodus died in the wilderness save Caleb and Joshua.

themes: Moses gave Gilead unto Machir the son of Manasseh; and he dwelt therein
the grant sealing their conquest
the inheritance passing to the new generation

schools.summary: Gill: Jair was of Manasseh by his mother's side, but of Judah by descent, through Hezron who married a daughter of Machir.
Clarke: Havoth-jair is 'the villages or habitations of Jair'; these tribes lay east of Jordan.

themes: Jair the son of Manasseh went and took the small towns thereof, and called them Havoth-jair
his conquest left as a memorial
the tribes mingled in his line

schools.summary: Gill: who Nobah was is uncertain, very probably a descendant of Manasseh, born in Egypt, who died after Moses beyond Jordan, as did Machir and Jair, none of that generation left but Caleb and Joshua.
Keil & Delitzsch: Nobah took Kenath and the villages thereof, and called it by his name.

themes: Nobah went and took Kenath... and called it Nobah, after his own name
the land settled by its conquerors
the old generation passing, none left save Caleb and Joshua
